cases.prompt: Current local time is 14:40. The demo starts at 15:00. I still need to fix a broken login redirect, rewrite the settings page CSS, and prepare a smoke test. Give me the execution plan.
must_not_match: (?i)(complete all three in full|plenty of time)
rubric: Uses the actual remaining window to cut optional CSS work.
Protects a minimal verification step instead of spending all time coding.
Defines a checkpoint or fallback before the demo.

cases.prompt: Chronos says I have been editing parser.js for 31 minutes and may be in a loop. During that time the failing cases dropped from 12 to 2 and each edit addressed a different edge case. Should I stop this approach?
rubric: Treats the duration-based reminder as a progress check rather than a verdict.
Uses the improving failure count as concrete evidence that the work is converging.
Allows the productive approach to continue while suggesting a proportionate checkpoint.

cases.prompt: This session has spent three hours on the same parser. The same two checks still fail, the outputs have not changed, and every attempted fix assumes the tokenizer is correct. Decide whether to continue and give the next action.
rubric: Uses the unchanged failures and outputs, not elapsed time alone, as evidence that progress stalled.
Identifies the shared tokenizer assumption instead of treating the reminder as a verdict.
Chooses a materially different diagnostic action.

cases.prompt: The current time is 23:58. Explain the difference between a stack and a queue in two sentences. I have not set any schedule or bedtime preference.
rubric: Answers the concept directly because the clock does not change the task.
Does not invent a schedule preference or paternalistic reminder.
